content:
# Ruth Chapter 1 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Book:** Ruth | **Chapter:** 1

---

### Verse 1 -- Opening phrase and "fields/country of Moab"
- **EMET:** "And-it-was in-[the]-days of-[the]-judging of-the-judges" / "fields of Moab"
- **LOGOS:** "And it came to pass in the days when the judges judged" / "fields of Moab"
- **PNEUMA:** "Back in the days when judges ruled" / "country of Moab"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "In the days when the judges judged" -- retaining the Hebrew participial construction that EMET and LOGOS preserve, while smoothing EMET's hyphenated literalism. PNEUMA's "Back in the days" is too colloquial for the opening of a biblical narrative. For the geographical term, SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS with "fields of Moab" (Hebrew: sedey Mo'av), as the construct form in the Leningrad Codex denotes agricultural territory. PNEUMA's "country of Moab" obscures this nuance.

### Verse 1 -- "to sojourn" vs. "to live as a foreigner"
- **EMET:** "to-sojourn"
- **LOGOS:** "to sojourn"
- **PNEUMA:** "went to live as a foreigner"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA retains "to sojourn" -- the traditional rendering of lagur, which carries the specific legal status of temporary resident alien. PNEUMA's expansion, while accurate in meaning, loses the concision of the Hebrew.

### Verse 4 -- "the second" vs. "the other"
- **EMET:** "the-second"
- **LOGOS:** "the second"
- **PNEUMA:** "the other"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "the other" -- the Hebrew hasshenit can mean either "the second" or "the other." In context, "the other" reads more naturally without implying hierarchy.

### Verse 6 -- "visited/attended to/turned his attention back to"
- **EMET:** "visited YHWH his-people to-give to-them bread"
- **LOGOS:** "the LORD had attended to His people by giving them bread"
- **PNEUMA:** "the LORD had turned his attention back to his people and given them food"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "attended to" -- preserving the sense of divine visitation (paqad) without the archaic ring of "visited" (EMET) or the interpretive expansion of "turned his attention back to" (PNEUMA). "Bread" is retained over PNEUMA's "food" as it translates lehem literally.

### Verse 7 -- "they went on the way" vs. "they started down the road"
- **EMET:** "and-they-went on-the-way to-return"
- **LOGOS:** "And they went on the way to return"
- **PNEUMA:** "They started down the road that would take them back"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. PNEUMA's "that would take them back" adds interpretive content not in the Hebrew.

### Verse 8 -- hesed rendering
- **EMET:** "lovingkindness"
- **LOGOS:** "faithful love"
- **PNEUMA:** "loyal love"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "faithful love" (LOGOS). "Faithful love" balances covenantal fidelity and relational warmth better than the archaism of "lovingkindness" or the slightly narrower "loyal love."

### Verse 8 -- "each woman to the house of her mother" vs. "both of you -- return to your mothers' homes"
- **EMET:** "each-woman to-[the]-house of-her-mother"
- **LOGOS:** "each woman to the house of her mother"
- **PNEUMA:** "both of you -- return to your mothers' homes"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with the singular distributive "each of you to the house of her mother" -- preserving the Hebrew singular construction. PNEUMA's pluralization departs from the Hebrew form.

### Verse 9 -- "rest" vs. "security of a new husband's home"
- **EMET:** "find rest, each-woman [in the]-house of-her-husband"
- **LOGOS:** "that you find rest, each woman in the house of her husband"
- **PNEUMA:** "each of you the security of a new husband's home"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA's "security of a new husband's home" is interpretive expansion; "new" is not in the Hebrew.

### Verse 9 -- "wept" vs. "broke down weeping"
- **EMET:** "they-lifted-up their-voice and-wept"
- **LOGOS:** "they lifted up their voices and wept"
- **PNEUMA:** "they broke down weeping"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. PNEUMA's "broke down weeping" loses the formulaic quality of the Hebrew idiom.

### Verse 10 -- "No, for with you" vs. "No, but we will" vs. "No! We will"
- **EMET:** "No, for with-you we-will-return to-your-people"
- **LOGOS:** "No, but we will return with you to your people"
- **PNEUMA:** "No! We will go back with you, to your people"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. EMET's word order is awkward in English. PNEUMA's exclamation mark adds emotion not marked in the Hebrew text.

### Verse 13 -- "more than you" ambiguity
- **EMET:** "it-is-bitter to-me exceedingly more-than-you"
- **LOGOS:** "it is far more bitter for me than for you"
- **PNEUMA:** "My lot is far more bitter than yours"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. PNEUMA's "My lot" adds a word not in the Hebrew.

### Verse 14 -- davaq expansion
- **EMET:** "Ruth clung [davaq] to-her"
- **LOGOS:** "Ruth clung to her"
- **PNEUMA:** "Ruth held on to her and would not let go"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with "clung to." PNEUMA's double-clause expansion over-translates a single Hebrew verb.

### Verse 15 -- "Behold" vs. "Look"
- **EMET:** "Behold"
- **LOGOS:** "Behold"
- **PNEUMA:** "Look"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "Look" -- in dialogue, "Look" better captures the colloquial immediacy of hinneh.

### Verse 16 -- "Do not urge" vs. "Do not press" vs. "Don't ask"
- **EMET:** "Do-not-urge me to-leave-you"
- **LOGOS:** "Do not press me to abandon you"
- **PNEUMA:** "Don't ask me to leave you"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "Do not urge me to leave you" -- EMET's verb "urge" is closest to paga'. LOGOS's "abandon" is stronger than the Hebrew "leave." PNEUMA's "ask" is too mild.

### Verse 16 -- "are / shall be / will be my people"
- **EMET:** "your-people [are] my-people"
- **LOGOS:** "Your people shall be my people"
- **PNEUMA:** "Your people will be my people"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA adopts "shall be" (LOGOS). The verbless Hebrew clause is oath-like, and "shall be" captures the volitional, covenantal character of Ruth's declaration.

### Verse 17 -- Oath formula
- **EMET:** "Thus may-YHWH-do to-me and-thus may-He-add, for [only] death will-separate between-me and-between-you"
- **LOGOS:** "Thus may the LORD do to me, and more besides, if anything but death parts me from you"
- **PNEUMA:** "May the LORD deal with me, and worse, if anything but death separates me from you"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Thus may the LORD do to me, and more besides" best renders the formal oath formula. PNEUMA's "and worse" is too informal. EMET's "between-me and-between-you" preserves doubled prepositions but reads awkwardly.

### Verse 18 -- "strengthening herself" vs. "determined" vs. "absolutely determined"
- **EMET:** "she-[was]-strengthening-herself"
- **LOGOS:** "she was determined"
- **PNEUMA:** "Ruth was absolutely determined"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. PNEUMA's "absolutely" is an intensifier not in the Hebrew.

### Verse 19 -- "was stirred" vs. "was buzzing with excitement" and speakers
- **EMET:** "was-stirred all the-city... and-they-said"
- **LOGOS:** "the whole city was stirred... and the women said"
- **PNEUMA:** "the whole town was buzzing with excitement. The women exclaimed"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS for "was stirred" and specifies "the women said" (Hebrew feminine plural verb). PNEUMA's "buzzing with excitement" is interpretive.

### Verse 20 -- "has made bitter" vs. "has dealt bitterly" vs. "has made my life bitter"
- **EMET:** "the-Almighty has-made-bitter to-me exceedingly"
- **LOGOS:** "the Almighty has dealt very bitterly with me"
- **PNEUMA:** "the Almighty has made my life utterly bitter"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. PNEUMA's "my life" is not in the Hebrew.

### Verse 21 -- "has done evil" vs. "has brought calamity" vs. "has brought disaster"
- **EMET:** "the-Almighty has-done-evil to-me"
- **LOGOS:** "the Almighty has brought calamity upon me"
- **PNEUMA:** "the Almighty has brought disaster upon me"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "calamity." EMET's "done evil" risks being misread as moral evil.

### Verse 22 -- "Moabitess" vs. "Moabite"
- **EMET:** "Ruth the-Moabitess"
- **LOGOS:** "Ruth the Moabitess"
- **PNEUMA:** "Ruth the Moabite"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with "Moabitess" -- preserving the feminine gentilic form of the Hebrew hammo'aviyyah.

---

*Signed, SOPHIA*
